category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Similar Questions/Part B/Linear Algebra — Inner Product and Orthogonal Complement

questiontext.text: The standard inner product on \(\mathbb{R}^n\) is \(\langle x,y\rangle=\sum_{i=1}^n x_iy_i\). Which property does it NOT generally satisfy for \(x\neq y\)?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (D) \(\langle x,y\rangle=\langle y,x\rangle^2\)

The standard inner product satisfies: (A) linearity in first argument, (B) symmetry \(\langle x,y\rangle=\langle y,x\rangle\), (C) positive definiteness \(\langle x,x\rangle\geq0\) with equality iff \(x=0\). It does NOT satisfy \(\langle x,y\rangle=\langle y,x\rangle^2\) in general — that would require the inner product to equal its own square, which is false.

questiontext.text: The Cauchy–Schwarz inequality states: for any inner product space, \(|\langle u,v\rangle|\leq\|u\|\cdot\|v\|\). Equality holds if and only if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(u\) and \(v\) are linearly dependent

Equality \(|\langle u,v\rangle|=\|u\|\|v\|\) iff \(u=\lambda v\) for some scalar \(\lambda\) (or one of them is zero). Geometrically: equality means the "angle" between \(u\) and \(v\) is 0 or \(\pi\), i.e., they are parallel. Cauchy–Schwarz is used to define angles in abstract inner product spaces.

questiontext.text: If \(W\) is a subspace of an inner product space \(V\), then \(V=W\oplus W^\perp\) means:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) Every \(v\in V\) has a unique decomposition \(v=w+w^\perp\) with \(w\in W\) and \(w^\perp\in W^\perp\)

Orthogonal decomposition theorem: for a closed subspace \(W\) of a Hilbert space, \(V=W\oplus W^\perp\). Every vector has a unique decomposition. The component \(w\) is the orthogonal projection of \(v\) onto \(W\). In finite dimensions, all subspaces are closed.

answer.text: Every \(v\in V\) has a unique decomposition \(v=w+w^\perp\) with \(w\in W,\;w^\perp\in W^\perp\)

questiontext.text: Apply Gram–Schmidt to \(\{(1,1,0),(1,0,1),(0,1,1)\}\) in \(\mathbb{R}^3\). The first two orthonormal vectors are \(e_1=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(1,1,0)\) and \(e_2=\):

On \(C[0,1]\), the inner product \(\langle f,g\rangle=\displaystyle\int_0^1 f(t)g(t)\,dt\). For the subspace \(W=\{f:f(0)=0\}\), the orthogonal complement \(W^\perp\) contains functions \(h\) satisfying:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (D) \(\int_0^1 h(t)f(t)\,dt=0\) for all \(f\in W\)

By definition: \(h\in W^\perp\Leftrightarrow\langle h,f\rangle=0\) for all \(f\in W\), i.e., \(\int_0^1 h(t)f(t)\,dt=0\) for all \(f\in W=\{f:f(0)=0\}\). In an infinite-dimensional setting, \(W^\perp\) may be trivial unless the inner product has special properties.

questiontext.text: If \(u\perp v\) in an inner product space, then:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(\|u+v\|^2=\|u\|^2+\|v\|^2\)

Pythagorean theorem: \(\|u+v\|^2=\langle u+v,u+v\rangle=\|u\|^2+2\langle u,v\rangle+\|v\|^2=\|u\|^2+\|v\|^2\) (since \(\langle u,v\rangle=0\)). This generalises to any orthogonal set: \(\|\sum_i u_i\|^2=\sum_i\|u_i\|^2\).

questiontext.text: A norm \(\|\cdot\|\) on a vector space comes from an inner product if and only if it satisfies the parallelogram law:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(\|u+v\|^2+\|u-v\|^2=2(\|u\|^2+\|v\|^2)\)

Jordan–von Neumann theorem: a norm is induced by an inner product iff it satisfies the parallelogram identity. If it does, the inner product is recovered by polarization: \(4\langle u,v\rangle=\|u+v\|^2-\|u-v\|^2\). The \(L^p\) norm for \(p\neq2\) fails this, explaining why \(L^2\) is special among \(L^p\) spaces.

questiontext.text: If \(\{e_1,e_2,\ldots\}\) is an orthonormal set in a Hilbert space and \(v\) is any vector, then Bessel's inequality states:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(\sum_{k=1}^\infty|\langle v,e_k\rangle|^2\leq\|v\|^2\)

Bessel's inequality: the sum of squared Fourier coefficients is bounded by the squared norm. Equality (Parseval's identity) holds iff \(\{e_k\}\) is a complete orthonormal system (ONB). The inequality follows from \(0\leq\|v-\sum_{k=1}^N\langle v,e_k\rangle e_k\|^2=\|v\|^2-\sum_{k=1}^N|\langle v,e_k\rangle|^2\).

questiontext.text: The element of a closed subspace \(W\) that is closest to \(v\in V\) (in the norm induced by the inner product) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) The orthogonal projection \(P_W v\)

Best approximation theorem: for a closed subspace \(W\) of a Hilbert space, the unique closest element to \(v\) in \(W\) is the orthogonal projection \(P_W v\), characterized by \(v-P_W v\perp W\). This is the foundation of least-squares, Fourier series, and optimal filtering.

answer.text: The vector in \(W\) with largest norm

questiontext.text: A linear operator \(T\) on an inner product space is self-adjoint if \(\langle Tu,v\rangle=\langle u,Tv\rangle\) for all \(u,v\). In matrix terms (for a real space), this means:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \([T]=[T]^T\) (symmetric matrix)

For a real inner product space with standard basis, self-adjoint = symmetric: \(\langle Ae_i,e_j\rangle=a_{ji}=\langle e_i,Ae_j\rangle=a_{ij}\). In complex case: self-adjoint = Hermitian (\(A=A^*=\bar{A}^T\)). Self-adjoint operators have real eigenvalues and orthogonal eigenspaces (spectral theorem).

questiontext.text: In a finite-dimensional inner product space \(V\) with \(\dim V=n\) and \(W\) a subspace with \(\dim W=k\), we have \(\dim W^\perp=\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(n-k\)

From the orthogonal decomposition \(V=W\oplus W^\perp\): \(\dim V=\dim W+\dim W^\perp\), so \(\dim W^\perp=n-k\). Also \((W^\perp)^\perp=W\). In infinite dimensions, \((W^\perp)^\perp\) equals the closure of \(W\), not \(W\) itself unless \(W\) is closed.

questiontext.text: A complex matrix \(U\) is unitary if \(U^*U=I\). The eigenvalues of a unitary matrix all satisfy:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(|\lambda|=1\)

If \(Uv=\lambda v\), then \(\|v\|^2=\|Uv\|^2=|\lambda|^2\|v\|^2\) (unitary matrices preserve norms), so \(|\lambda|=1\). Unitary matrices generalize orthogonal matrices to \(\mathbb{C}\): they preserve the complex inner product. All eigenvalues lie on the unit circle.

questiontext.text: The Fourier coefficient of \(f\in L^2[-\pi,\pi]\) with respect to the orthonormal function \(e_n(x)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{inx}\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(\hat{f}(n)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\int_{-\pi}^\pi f(x)e^{-inx}dx\)

The inner product \(\langle f,e_n\rangle=\int_{-\pi}^\pi f(x)\overline{e_n(x)}dx=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\int_{-\pi}^\pi f(x)e^{-inx}dx\). These are the Fourier coefficients. Parseval's identity: \(\sum_{n=-\infty}^\infty|\hat{f}(n)|^2=\|f\|^2=\int_{-\pi}^\pi|f|^2dx\).

questiontext.text: The orthogonal projection of \(v=(1,2,3)\) onto the subspace spanned by \(u=(1,0,0)\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \((1,0,0)\)

\(\text{proj}_u v=\frac{\langle v,u\rangle}{\langle u,u\rangle}u=\frac{1}{1}(1,0,0)=(1,0,0)\). The residual \(v-\text{proj}_u v=(0,2,3)\perp(1,0,0)\) ✓. The orthogonal projection formula: \(P_u=\frac{uu^T}{\|u\|^2}\).

questiontext.text: An orthonormal set \(\{e_k\}\) in a Hilbert space \(H\) is complete (i.e., an ONB) if and only if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(\|v\|^2=\sum_k|\langle v,e_k\rangle|^2\) for all \(v\in H\) (Parseval's identity)

An ONS is complete iff Parseval's identity holds iff the only vector orthogonal to all \(e_k\) is 0 iff span\(\{e_k\}\) is dense in \(H\). These are all equivalent characterizations of an orthonormal basis (ONB) in a Hilbert space.

answer.text: Parseval's identity: \(\|v\|^2=\sum_k|\langle v,e_k\rangle|^2\) for all \(v\in H\)
All \(e_k\) are orthogonal to each other

questiontext.text: An operator \(T\) is normal if \(TT^*=T^*T\). Which matrix is normal?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(\begin{pmatrix}1&i\\-i&1\end{pmatrix}\)

A matrix \(A\) is normal iff \(AA^*=A^*A\). For \(A=\begin{pmatrix}1&i\\-i&1\end{pmatrix}\): \(A^*=\begin{pmatrix}1&i\\-i&1\end{pmatrix}=A\) (Hermitian!), so \(AA^*=A^2=A^*A\) ✓. Hermitian, unitary, and skew-Hermitian matrices are all normal. Normal matrices are unitarily diagonalizable (complex spectral theorem).

questiontext.text: In any inner product space, the triangle inequality \(\|u+v\|\leq\|u\|+\|v\|\) follows from:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) The Cauchy–Schwarz inequality

\(\|u+v\|^2=\|u\|^2+2\langle u,v\rangle+\|v\|^2\leq\|u\|^2+2|\langle u,v\rangle|+\|v\|^2\leq\|u\|^2+2\|u\|\|v\|+\|v\|^2=(\|u\|+\|v\|)^2\). The key step uses Cauchy–Schwarz: \(|\langle u,v\rangle|\leq\|u\|\|v\|\).

questiontext.text: The Riesz representation theorem states: every bounded linear functional \(\phi\) on a Hilbert space \(H\) is of the form:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(\phi(v)=\langle v,u\rangle\) for a unique \(u\in H\)

Riesz representation: every continuous linear functional on a Hilbert space is given by inner product with a unique fixed vector. This establishes the identification \(H\cong H^*\) for Hilbert spaces — they are self-dual. It is the foundation of the weak formulation in PDEs and quantum mechanics (bra-ket notation).

question.single: truetrueabc \(\phi(v)=\|v\|\) for all \(v\) \(\phi(v)=\|v\|^2\) \(\phi(v)=\langle v,u\rangle\) for a unique \(u\in H\) \(\phi(v)=0\) for all \(v\perp u\)

questiontext.text: In \(P_2\) with inner product \(\langle p,q\rangle=\int_0^1 p(t)q(t)\,dt\), the orthogonal complement of \(\{1\}\) (constant functions) consists of:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) Polynomials \(p\) with \(\int_0^1 p(t)\,dt=0\)

\(p\perp\{1\}\Leftrightarrow\langle p,1\rangle=\int_0^1 p(t)\cdot1\,dt=0\). In \(P_2\), this is satisfied by e.g. \(p(t)=t-\frac{1}{2}\) and \(q(t)=t^2-\frac{1}{3}\) (after removing the mean). These are related to Legendre polynomials on \([0,1]\).

questiontext.text: In \(\mathbb{R}^3\) with standard inner product, let \(W=\{(x,y,z):x+y+z=0\}\). Then \(\dim(W^\perp)=\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) 1

\(W\) is a 2-dimensional subspace (kernel of the linear functional \((x,y,z)\mapsto x+y+z\)). By the orthogonal decomposition: \(\dim W^\perp=3-\dim W=3-2=1\). Explicitly, \(W^\perp=\mathrm{span}\{(1,1,1)\}\) (the normal to the plane \(x+y+z=0\)).
